items.correct: Yes — plants respire all the time to release energy; they also photosynthesise when there is light.
items.misconception: Plants only photosynthesise and do not respire; respiration is for animals only.
items.random_wrong: Plants only respire at night and only photosynthesise during the day; the processes never overlap.
items.why: Because photosynthesis is taught as the plant equivalent of respiration, students think it replaces respiration in plants entirely.

items.q: Where do plants get most of their biomass from?
items.correct: From carbon dioxide in the air — the carbon in plant tissue comes mostly from CO2 fixed during photosynthesis.
items.misconception: Plants get most of their mass from the soil through their roots.
items.random_wrong: Plants gain most of their mass directly from sunlight, which has weight.
items.why: Roots in soil look like the source of food/mass, but actually most plant dry mass is carbon from atmospheric CO2.

items.q: Why is ATP described as an 'energy currency' rather than energy storage?
items.correct: ATP is constantly made and broken down — it transfers energy between reactions rather than storing large amounts for later.
items.misconception: Cells store large amounts of ATP for emergency use.
items.random_wrong: ATP is permanent and accumulates with age in cells.
items.why: Students treat ATP as a battery that fills up and gets used; in reality, cells make ATP just-in-time and have only seconds' worth at any moment.

items.q: In the pondweed experiment, is the number of bubbles the independent variable?
items.correct: No — the bubble rate is the dependent variable (what you measure); the lamp distance (light intensity) is the independent variable.
items.misconception: The number of bubbles per minute is the variable the student changes.
items.random_wrong: The temperature of the water is the dependent variable here.
items.why: Students mix up the variables; you change the light (independent) and measure the resulting bubble rate (dependent), keeping temperature constant.

items.q: Do plants in the dark stop respiring?
items.correct: No — plants respire continuously, day and night; only photosynthesis stops in the dark.
items.misconception: Plants stop respiring when there is no light.
items.random_wrong: Plants respire only when they are photosynthesising.
items.why: Students tie all plant gas exchange to light, but respiration releases energy at all times; in the dark, only respiration occurs.

items.q: In the pondweed practical, what is the INDEPENDENT variable?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Distance of the lamp. “Number of bubbles per minute” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Why is sodium hydrogencarbonate added to the water?
items.correct: To provide a steady supply of CO₂
items.why: Correct answer: To provide a steady supply of CO₂. “To kill bacteria” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: You count 45 oxygen bubbles in 3 minutes. What is the rate in bubbles per minute?
items.why: Exact answer: 15. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
